title: Fortnite chapter 7 season 1 players blast Epic for using AI art
text:
Follow

By
Chris Hayner
Published
Dec 1, 2025, 12:37 PM EST
News
Fans claim Epic Games is using generative AI art this season, though one artist has debunked a claim
Related
Ghibli-inspired D&D campaign setting is on sale for Cyber Monday
Adventure Time showrunner explains what that wild Fionna and Cake death means for the lore
‘All I wanted was to save my family’: How Alex Woo turned a childhood fear into a hit Netflix movie
Fortnite chapter 7 kicks off new controversy over AI art
Image: Epic Games
Fortnite Chapter 7, Season 1
Fortnite
Sign in to your
Polygon.com
account
A new chapter of
Fortnite
is here. With it comes a
new map and loot pool
, as well as several
small upgrades
to make for a smoother player experience. According to some of those players, though, there was another change made to
Fortnite
. A contingent of fans are calling out Epic Games with claims that generative AI was used in the creation of some of the game's new art.
The drama unfolded over the weekend, as players on
Reddit
called out multiple pieces of in-game art for potentially being made using AI. The first piece that caught the attention of an eagle-eyed player is a poster for the fictional movie
Mile High Retreat
, which features a nine-toed yeti — five toes on one foot, four on the other. Inaccurate numbers of fingers and toes have become a hallmark of AI-generated art.
One accusation was quickly debunked. A
Back to the Future 2
-themed spray featuring an animated, almost-Ghibli-esque image of Marty McFly was called out by players as generated by AI, before the artist stepped forward.
"I guess someone on Reddit thinks this was AI," illustrator Sean Dove wrote on
Instagram
. "I think the culprit is a clock in the background. I grabbed some clocks off image search, collaged them, and halftoned them. The numbers are bad, entirely possible I grabbed an AI clock [and] wasn’t paying attention."
Dove followed that post with a compilation of the layers he used in Procreate, the graphic design app. You can see that below.
The question of AI use in
Fortnite
comes not long after Epic Games CEO Tim Sweeney shared his stance on AI disclosures on game stores like Valve's Steam, writing on social media that he thinks such disclosures have no place on storefronts.
"The AI tag is relevant to art exhibits for authorship disclosure, and to digital content licensing marketplaces where buyers need to understand the rights situation," he wrote on
X
. "It makes no sense for game stores, where AI will be involved in nearly all future production."
He later followed up on that comment in a reply to a follower who asked if Epic is using AI coding tools in Unreal Engine. According to Sweeney, the use of AI coding tools at Epic Games is "not true." Further, he
wrote
, "I just hate to see Valve confiscate ever more opportunity from small developers by facilitating new categories cancel campaigns [
sic
] and review bombing. Steam used to just facilitate downloads. Then they foreclosed on payments, then price competition, then crypto, now AI."
Of course, none of this clears up whether generative AI was used in the creation of the game's art assets. The answer to that question will ultimately have to come from Epic.
Polygon has reached out to Epic Games for comment on the issue of whether generative AI is used in the latest
Fortnite
chapter and will update this story when the company responds.
